text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for oncology follow-up; assessment of response to enzalutamide in the setting of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Approximately 6 months since enzalutamide initiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents today for routine follow-up approximately 6 months after initiating enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily. He was originally diagnosed with stage IVB (M1b) acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate approximately 2.5 years ago at age 67, with Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 disease. Initial PSA was 187 ng/mL. He was found at diagnosis to have bilateral pelvic lymph node involvement and 8 discrete bone metastases (L2, T9, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs). Germline testing at diagnosis confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 p.E1308* variant. PTEN loss on IHC and somatic RB1 deletion were also noted.

He was treated with first-line ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM Q3 months) plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID for metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC), achieving an excellent PSA nadir of 1.1 ng/mL. He had a sustained response for approximately 14 months before developing biochemical progression, confirmed by two consecutive rising PSA values. Restaging imaging at the time of CRPC transition demonstrated progression with new bone lesions (total 12), enlarging pelvic lymph nodes, and new retroperitoneal nodes. He was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily (abiraterone discontinued, ADT continued) at that time.

Two months into enzalutamide therapy, he developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 subjective cognitive slowing, with mild hypertension that required uptitration of his antihypertensives (amlodipine increased to 10 mg, lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily). No seizure activity. No falls. These side effects were managed conservatively and improved over subsequent months.

Today, at approximately 6 months on enzalutamide, Mr. Kowalski reports the following:
- Back pain: substantially improved. He previously rated lumbar pain at 3/10 at the time of CRPC diagnosis; currently 1/10, well-controlled on acetaminophen 1g TID as needed (no opioids required).
- Fatigue: ongoing grade 1-2, improved from peak grade 2-3 seen at the 2-month mark. He describes it as manageable and has been able to perform household activities, light yard work, and attend family events.
- Hot flashes: ongoing, grade 1-2, attributed to ongoing ADT. He uses a fan at night and has adapted his sleep routine.
- Cognitive symptoms: mild subjective word-finding difficulty persists at grade 1. Family confirms no significant functional impairment. He continues to manage his own finances and medications.
- No new bone pain, no neurological symptoms, no lower extremity weakness or bowel/bladder dysfunction to suggest cord compromise.
- Appetite: good. No significant weight change. Diet well-maintained.
- No dyspnea, no chest pain, no hemoptysis.
- No lower urinary tract symptoms. No hematuria.
- Bowel function: normal.
- Ambulating without assistance.

He has been compliant with enzalutamide (confirmed by pill count and patient report). He takes the 4 x 40 mg capsules together once daily in the morning with food. He denies missing more than 1-2 doses over the 6-month period.

Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV Q12 weeks was initiated at this visit or prior (bone-directed therapy), dose-adjusted for chronic kidney disease (eGFR ~48). He takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation. He was counseled on osteonecrosis of the jaw and dental hygiene; dental clearance is on file.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2 as noted. No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Appetite stable. No significant weight loss.
Neurological: Grade 1 cognitive slowing (subjective). No seizures. No headaches. No focal weakness or sensory deficits. No visual changes.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension, currently well-controlled on adjusted regimen. No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema (mild, grade 1, unchanged).
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or with exertion. No cough.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Bowel habits regular.
Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. No hematuria. No dysuria. Libido absent (expected given ADT).
Musculoskeletal: Mild residual low back discomfort, 1/10, stable. No new joint pain. No fracture symptoms.
Hematologic: No bruising, bleeding, or signs of infection.
Endocrine: Hot flashes, grade 1-2. No polydipsia or polyuria beyond baseline (diabetes well-controlled per patient).
Skin: No rash. No hair changes beyond expected from ADT.
Psychiatric: Mild low mood acknowledged. Denies depression, anxiety, or suicidal ideation. Supported by wife and adult children.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone and lymph node metastases -- as described above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- on metformin (held when contrast given)
3. Hypertension -- on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily
4. Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a-3b -- baseline creatinine 1.4-1.6 mg/dL, eGFR ~48-52; monitored closely
5. Prior tobacco use: 45 pack-year smoking history; quit at age 62

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Transrectal ultrasound-guided 12-core prostate biopsy (diagnostic, at age 67)
- No prostatectomy; patient was not a surgical candidate given metastatic presentation

FAMILY HISTORY
- Brother: pancreatic cancer, age 58 (deceased)
- Father: prostate cancer, age 74 (deceased)
- No known maternal malignancies reported
- Germline BRCA2 p.E1308* pathogenic variant confirmed in patient; family genetic counseling referral placed previously

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Retired steelworker, Polish-American descent
- Married, lives with wife; adult children involved in care
- Former smoker: 45 pack-years, quit age 62
- No alcohol use currently
- No illicit drug use
- Functional at baseline -- manages ADLs independently; limited IADLs due to fatigue

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS
1. Enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg (4 x 40 mg capsules) orally once daily
2. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next injection due in approximately 6 weeks)
3. Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (for skeletal metastases, dose-adjusted for CKD)
4. Prednisone 5 mg BID -- NOTE: prednisone was initially part of the abiraterone regimen; need to clarify whether continued; patient reports he was told to stop this when abiraterone was discontinued [to be reviewed and discontinued if still on it]
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
6. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily
7. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
8. Acetaminophen 1000 mg orally TID as needed (for bone pain)
9. Calcium carbonate 1250 mg (500 mg elemental calcium) orally twice daily
10. Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2000 IU orally daily
11. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (GI prophylaxis)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 138/82 mmHg (left arm, sitting)
- Heart rate: 72 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- Temperature: 98.4°F (36.9°C)
- Weight: 182 lbs (82.6 kg); BMI 26.4 (down approximately 4 lbs from prior visit, likely fluid shift)
- SpO2: 97% on room air
- ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing, appropriately dressed, in no acute distress. Conversational. Mild slowing noted in verbal responses, consistent with reported cognitive side effect.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions or signs of mucositis. No lymphadenopathy of the neck or supraclavicular fossa palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Good air entry.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: Mild bilateral lower extremity edema, grade 1 (pretibial, pitting). No calf tenderness. No skin breakdown.

Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion of all extremities without significant pain. Spine: mild tenderness to deep palpation over lower lumbar spinous processes (L2-L4), unchanged from prior. No point tenderness over ribs, pelvis, or femurs. Gait: steady, no assistive device.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Deep tendon reflexes symmetric. No focal neurological deficits.

Genitourinary/Rectal: Deferred at this visit given recent imaging and ongoing systemic therapy; no new urinary complaints.

Skin: No rash. No ecchymoses. No pallor appreciated on exam (though mild anemia present on labs).

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or cervical lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Oncologic markers:
- PSA: 1.4 ng/mL [decline of 83.9% from enzalutamide baseline of 8.7 ng/mL; nadir appears to be continuing to approach]
- Testosterone: 19 ng/dL [confirmed castrate-level suppression]

Complete blood count:
- WBC: 5.6 K/uL
- ANC: 3.4 K/uL
- Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL [stable mild anemia; previously 10.9 g/dL at enzalutamide initiation]
- MCV: 88 fL
- Platelets: 178,000/uL

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 26 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL [mild increase from baseline ~1.4; eGFR ~48 by CKD-EPI]
- eGFR (CKD-EPI): 48 mL/min/1.73m2 [stable CKD stage 3b; above threshold for zoledronic acid dose adjustment protocol]
- Glucose (fasting): 118 mg/dL [reasonably controlled; HbA1c 6.9% from 4 weeks prior]
- Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL [normal, on calcium/Vit D supplementation; appropriate given zoledronic acid use]
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L
- ALT: 22 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): 112 U/L [borderline elevated; was 187 at initial diagnosis, normalized to 98 at mCSPC response, now mild uptick from residual osseous disease -- stable from last check]
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- LDH: 186 U/L [within normal range]

Other:
- HbA1c: 6.9% (from 4 weeks prior)
- PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)
- Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging bone scan and CT of the abdomen/pelvis performed approximately one month prior:

Bone scan: Compared to the scan obtained at the time of CRPC progression (which showed 12 lesions, several new), there is overall improvement in osseous disease activity. The T12 vertebral body lesion and left femoral head lesion, both new at prior study, now show partial response with decreased tracer uptake. The right femoral neck lesion is stable. The previously progressive lesions in the left iliac wing and right acetabulum show decreased tracer intensity. No new foci of abnormal osseous uptake identified.

CT abdomen/pelvis: Pelvic and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy has regressed. The right obturator lymph node measures 0.9 cm, down from 2.1 cm at CRPC progression. The left common iliac node is 0.8 cm, down from 1.2 cm. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy. No hepatic lesions identified. No pulmonary nodules on the abdominal windows (full chest CT not performed at this restaging). No adrenal masses. Prostate/seminal vesicle region shows post-treatment changes without new concerning masses.

Overall: The imaging findings are consistent with a meaningful partial response to enzalutamide in both osseous and nodal disease compartments.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, Gleason 9 (5+4), with germline BRCA2 p.E1308* and somatic PTEN/RB1 loss, who is now approximately 6 months into enzalutamide therapy following progression on ADT + abiraterone.

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER -- RESPONDING TO ENZALUTAMIDE

He has had an excellent response to enzalutamide. PSA has declined from 8.7 ng/mL at the time of enzalutamide initiation to the current value of 1.4 ng/mL, representing an 83.9% decline. Testosterone is confirmed at castrate levels (19 ng/dL). Restaging imaging demonstrates partial response in both bone and lymph node disease with no new lesions identified. Clinically, his back pain has improved substantially and his performance status is maintained at ECOG 1.

Plan:
- Continue enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. No dose adjustment at this time.
- Continue ADT with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM Q3 months; next injection to be scheduled.
- Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV Q12 weeks (next infusion in approximately 8 weeks). Monitor renal function and calcium closely.
- Repeat PSA and testosterone in 8-10 weeks.
- Restaging imaging (bone scan + CT) in approximately 6 months or sooner if PSA rises significantly or new symptoms arise.
- Closely monitor for enzalutamide-related adverse effects, particularly cognitive and cardiovascular effects.

2. ENZALUTAMIDE-RELATED ADVERSE EFFECTS

The patient experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 cognitive slowing early in the course, managed conservatively. Fatigue has improved to grade 1-2. Cognitive symptoms remain mild (grade 1) and non-functionally limiting.

Hypertension was uptitrated (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg) and is currently reasonably controlled today at 138/82 mmHg. Will continue to monitor at each visit.

Plan:
- Fatigue: counsel on moderate aerobic exercise (walking program), sleep hygiene, and pacing. No dose reduction required.
- Cognitive symptoms: continue to monitor. Neuropsychological referral to be considered if worsens. Counseled patient and wife to report any new cognitive changes promptly, and to monitor for falls or balance issues.
- No seizure prophylaxis indicated currently.
- Falls precautions reviewed (bathroom grab bars, clear pathways at home). Patient has a dog that he is aware of as a trip hazard.

3. ANEMIA -- CHRONIC, MULTIFACTORIAL

Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL today, mildly down from 10.9 g/dL at enzalutamide initiation but stable over recent months. Likely related to ADT effects on erythropoiesis and underlying chronic disease/CKD. No acute bleeding source identified.

Plan:
- Monitor CBC with each lab check. No transfusion indicated at this time.
- Iron studies: will send at next visit to evaluate for any correctable component. Currently on no iron supplementation.
- If hemoglobin drops below 9.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will reassess including consideration of hematology referral.

4. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (Stage 3b, eGFR ~48)

Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, eGFR 48 mL/min. Mild worsening from baseline of 1.4 mg/dL; not acutely concerning. Continue to monitor closely, particularly given zoledronic acid use and contrast exposures. Metformin: patient is currently using metformin -- given eGFR 48, this is at the lower range of acceptable use; will continue with close monitoring but should hold if eGFR falls below 30 or if patient receives contrast.

Plan:
- BMP with each oncology visit.
- Hold metformin 48 hours before and after any IV contrast administration.
- Zoledronic acid dose: patient is receiving 4 mg Q12 weeks (reduced from standard Q4 weeks). Per institutional guidelines, the dose is appropriate given eGFR 40-60 range. Will reassess if eGFR declines further.

5. DIABETES MELLITUS TYPE 2 -- REASONABLY CONTROLLED

HbA1c 6.9% (from 4 weeks prior). Fasting glucose 118 mg/dL today. Well-controlled. Note: corticosteroid use (prednisone carried over from abiraterone era) may contribute to hyperglycemia -- confirm below whether prednisone should be discontinued.

IMPORTANT NOTE REGARDING PREDNISONE: Prednisone 5 mg BID was co-prescribed with abiraterone. Abiraterone was discontinued approximately 3 months ago when enzalutamide was initiated. It is not standard practice to continue prednisone with enzalutamide. Medication reconciliation performed today: patient confirmed he is no longer taking prednisone (was discontinued by the covering pharmacist at the time of transition -- confirmed). Medication list updated accordingly.

6. HYPERTENSION

Currently on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 10 mg. BP today 138/82 mmHg. Reasonable control. No change needed today.

7. GERMLINE BRCA2 STATUS AND FUTURE TREATMENT PLANNING

As discussed at prior visits, the patient carries a germline pathogenic BRCA2 p.E1308* variant, with loss of heterozygosity in the tumor. This is a therapeutically actionable alteration. He was previously informed about olaparib (a PARP inhibitor, Lynparza) and its efficacy in BRCA2-mutant mCRPC per the PROfound trial data.

At this time, enzalutamide is providing good disease control. Olaparib remains a priority option upon enzalutamide progression. In the event of confirmed biochemical or radiographic progression on enzalutamide, the transition plan would be to discontinue enzalutamide and initiate olaparib 300 mg BID.

Plan:
- Continue enzalutamide as long as effective.
- Remind patient and family of olaparib as the intended next-line therapy given BRCA2 mutation.
- Genetic counseling: recommend family members (children) pursue BRCA2 testing given germline nature; referral letter provided.

8. BONE HEALTH

Patient remains on zoledronic acid Q12 weeks. Calcium and Vitamin D supplementation ongoing. Dental hygiene counseled -- patient has maintained regular dental care and no procedures are planned.

No pathological fractures identified on imaging. Will continue current approach. Orthopedic surgery was previously consulted regarding right femoral neck lesion, which has responded to enzalutamide and does not require surgical intervention at this time.

---

RETURN VISIT

Follow-up in 8-10 weeks for PSA and labs. Zoledronic acid infusion scheduled in approximately 8 weeks. Patient instructed to call or present to urgent care/ER promptly for any new neurological symptoms (back pain with radiation, leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction), fever, or significant bleeding.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of encounter spent in counseling and coordination.

---

Electronically signed by:
[GU Oncology Attending]
Genitourinary Oncology
[Institution Name]
[Contact: GU Oncology Clinic]
